data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was caused by the exhaustion of Cypriot copper mines that underwrote the entire Eastern Mediterranean military-technological complex, with regional polities falling sequentially as their strategic bronze inventories depleted at differential rates","mechanism": "Open-cast mining at Apliki and other Cypriot ore bodies reached geological limits c. 1250 BC; falling copper-bronze availability raised relative prices of military equipment above the fiscal extractive capacity of palace systems; Mycenaean wanax economies, most dependent on chariot aristocracies, collapsed first; Hittite iron-poor Anatolia followed; Egypt survived through Nubian gold purchases of residual Cypriot output and early adoption of carburized iron","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus", "bronze metallurgical economies"], "excludes": ["climate causation", "volcanic mechanisms", "Sea Peoples as primary cause", "network systemic theories"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Lead isotope or trace element evidence showing sustained Cypriot copper production through 1200-1150 BC without interruption", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence of intensified rather than diminished Cypriot mining activity in the decades before collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Equal or greater collapse severity in iron-rich polities (e.g., some Anatolian highlands) versus copper-dependent lowland palaces", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian textual evidence of bronze rather than gold as the primary medium of Nubian exchange in the 19th-20th Dynasties", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeometric evidence that early Egyptian iron tools predated or were independent of Cypriot bronze exhaustion", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "More typical materialist candidate testing peak-resource theory against the Bronze Age; vulnerable to chronological mismatches between mining exhaustion and collapse, and to the well-documented continuity of Cypriot polity; exploits genuine debate about Bronze-Iron transition timing"}
